title: Show confirmation before destructive data operations
summary: Generated interfaces execute destructive actions — delete, archive, remove, ban, clear — immediately without confirmation, undo option, or visual acknowledgment, putting user data one accidental click away from loss.
instruction: Every destructive action (delete, archive, remove, clear, reset, ban, terminate) must be preceded by a confirmation dialog that states the action, the item name, and the consequence. Critical actions (permanent delete, data wipe, account termination) require typed confirmation (e.g., type DELETE). Non-critical but destructive actions must offer an undo mechanism (toast with undo button) for at least 5 seconds.
rationale: Undo and confirmation are the primary safeguards against accidental data loss. Without confirmation, a single misclick can destroy weeks of work. Gmail undo-send feature demonstrated that users make mistakes within the 5-second window 30% of the time. Confirmation dialogs reduce accidental deletions by an estimated 70-90%.
violations: A file manager where clicking a Delete icon removes the file immediately with no confirmation dialog and no undo toast.
A user management dashboard where a single click on an X button deletes a user account permanently with no confirmation dialog and no undo.
A form builder where clicking Clear All immediately resets all fields without confirmation and with no undo option.
controls: A file manager where clicking Delete opens a dialog: Delete report.pdf? This action can be undone for 30 days. with Cancel and Move to Trash buttons.
A user management dashboard where clicking Deactivate shows a dialog: Deactivate user john@example.com? They will be unable to log in. with Cancel and Confirm Deactivation styled as destructive red.
A form where clicking Clear All shows a confirmation toast stating All fields cleared with an Undo button that persists for 5 seconds.
borderlineCases: A 'Remove from list' action on a non-critical item (e.g., dismissing a notification) where an undo toast is sufficient and a dialog would be overkill.
contextualExceptions: Power-user interfaces where bulk operations execute without confirmation and a separate undo panel is available (e.g., Gmail archive, Trello deletion with undo banner).
APIs and developer tools where the command-line or API call itself is the explicit confirmation of intent.
Temporary state changes (hiding an element, collapsing a section) that are not data-destructive.
detectorSignal: Buttons or links with text matching destructive action patterns (delete, remove, clear, erase, archive, ban, terminate) that are not preceded by a modal or dialog element within 2 DOM levels
matcherProposal: Identify interactive elements whose text content matches action verbs: delete, remove, clear, erase, archive, ban, terminate, destroy, purge, reset (to default). For each, check the next 2 DOM levels for: (a) a sibling dialog or modal with confirmation text, or (b) a subsequent toast container with an undo action. Flag if none detected.
detectorLimitations: Cannot detect confirmation implemented through JavaScript event handlers (confirm(), custom confirm dialog) without runtime code analysis.
Destructive actions triggered by drag-and-drop, keyboard shortcuts, or context menus are invisible to static button-text analysis.
Undo-toast mechanisms that appear only after the action executes cannot be detected pre-emptively.
falsePositiveScenarios: A Clear filters button that resets search filters (not data) without confirmation — matches the heuristic pattern but is not data-destructive.
A Remove from cart action in an e-commerce interface — technically removes an item but is undoable via Add again without special infrastructure.
An action that triggers a server-side soft delete (recoverable) with a built-in undo window not visible client-side.
mitigationStrategy: Only flag actions that involve data entities (files, users, records, posts, messages, accounts) where the class or surrounding context indicates a data object. Exclude UI-state toggles, filter clears, and cart removals.

fixturePlans.violation: A file manager page where each file row has a red Delete icon that immediately removes the file with no confirmation dialog, no toast, and no undo option.
fixturePlans.control: A file manager where clicking Delete opens a modal: Move quarterly-report.pdf to Trash? You can restore it within 30 days. with Cancel (ghost) and Move to Trash buttons.
fixturePlans.borderline: A Gmail-style interface where clicking the archive icon immediately archives the conversation, but an undo banner appears at the top for 7 seconds — no dialog, but undo is available.

cleanRoomAttestations: Developed without access to proprietary prompt collections.
Undo and confirmation effectiveness statistics based on publicly available UX research.
overlapNotes: First data-integrity proposal. No overlap with existing rules.
